name: Acne
description: Acne is a common skin condition that occurs when hair follicles become clogged with oil and dead skin cells, leading to pimples, blackheads, and whiteheads.
symptoms: Whiteheads (clogged pores)
Blackheads (open clogged pores)
Small red, tender bumps (papules)
Pimples (pustules) with pus at the tips
Large, solid, painful lumps beneath the skin (nodules)
Painful, pus-filled lumps beneath the skin (cystic lesions)
causes: Hair follicles clogged by oil and dead skin cells
Diet and stress (can trigger or worsen)
precautions: Avoid picking, squeezing, or popping pimples, which can cause scarring or infection.
Wash your face twice daily with a gentle, non-comedogenic cleanser.
Choose makeup and skincare products labeled 'oil-free' or 'non-comedogenic'.
Protect skin from excessive sun exposure.
homeCare: Keep skin clean and avoid vigorous scrubbing.
Apply over-the-counter topical treatments containing salicylic acid, benzoyl peroxide, or adapalene.
Wash hair regularly, especially if it is oily and rests against your face.
Use a light, oil-free moisturizer to prevent skin dryness.
disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice or a diagnosis. Please consult a qualified dermatologist for personal concerns.

name: Eczema
description: Eczema (atopic dermatitis) is a condition that makes your skin dry, red, itchy, and irritated. It is common in children but can occur at any age.
symptoms: Dry skin
Severe itching, especially at night
Red to brownish-gray patches, especially on the hands, feet, ankles, wrists, neck, and upper chest
Small, raised bumps which may leak fluid and crust over when scratched
Thickened, cracked, or scaly skin
Raw, sensitive, swollen skin from scratching
causes: Genetics and family history of eczema, asthma, or allergies
Skin barrier dysfunction allowing moisture out and irritants in
precautions: Moisturize your skin at least twice a day using creams or ointments.
Avoid harsh soaps, detergents, and synthetic fabrics.
Identify and avoid triggers that worsen the inflammation.
Keep fingernails short to prevent skin damage from scratching.
homeCare: Take warm (not hot) baths or showers, limited to 10-15 minutes.
Apply moisturizer immediately after bathing while the skin is damp.
Apply cool, wet compresses to soothe itchy skin.
disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice or a diagnosis. Please consult a qualified dermatologist for personal concerns.

name: Psoriasis
description: Psoriasis is a chronic autoimmune skin disease that speeds up the growth cycle of skin cells, leading to thick, red, scaly patches that are often itchy and painful.
symptoms: Red patches of skin covered with thick, silvery scales
Small scaling spots (commonly seen in children)
Dry, cracked skin that may bleed or itch
Itching, burning, or soreness
Thickened, pitted, or ridged nails
Swollen and stiff joints
causes: Autoimmune system malfunction where T-cells attack healthy skin cells
Environmental triggers (infections, skin injuries, stress, cold weather, smoking)
precautions: Avoid skin injuries like cuts, scrapes, or severe sunburns, which can trigger new patches (Koebner phenomenon).
Limit alcohol consumption and avoid smoking.
Manage stress levels through relaxation techniques.
Avoid dry and cold environments if possible.
homeCare: Apply heavy moisturizers or ointments regularly to keep skin lubricated.
Take daily baths with Epsom salts, oil, or colloidal oatmeal to soothe skin.
Get moderate amounts of sunlight (ensure sun protection on unaffected skin).
Avoid scratching or picking at scales.
disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice or a diagnosis. Please consult a qualified dermatologist for personal concerns.

name: Fungal Infection
description: Fungal skin infections (like ringworm, athlete's foot, or jock itch) are common infections caused by microscopic fungi that thrive in warm, moist areas of the body.
symptoms: Red, itchy, or scaly circular rash with raised edges (ringworm)
Itching, burning, and stinging between toes (athlete's foot)
Chafing, redness, or burning in the groin area (jock itch)
Peeling, cracking, or flaking skin
Blisters in affected areas
causes: Overgrowth of dermatophytes or yeasts on the skin
Direct skin-to-skin contact with an infected person or animal
Sharing contaminated items like towels, clothing, or hairbrushes
Warm, humid environments and poor ventilation (tight shoes, sweaty clothes)
precautions: Keep skin clean and dry, especially in skin folds, between toes, and groin.
Avoid sharing towels, clothing, shoes, or sports gear.
Wear sandals or flip-flops in public showers, locker rooms, and pool areas.
homeCare: Apply over-the-counter topical antifungal creams, sprays, or powders.
Dry the affected area completely after bathing before applying treatments.
Wash infected clothing, bedding, and towels frequently in hot water.
disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice or a diagnosis. Please consult a qualified dermatologist for personal concerns.
